points.text: Assign a monotonically increasing sequence number to each event per client; drop any received with a lower or duplicate number.
Include a per‑connection token (e.g., JWT with short TTL) in every WebSocket frame and verify it server‑side to prevent replay.
On reconnect, have the client send the last sequence it processed; the server resumes from the next number, avoiding gaps.
Use idempotency keys (e.g., event UUID) and a short‑term cache to discard duplicate events arriving out of order.
Implement ACK/NACK flow: client acknowledges received seq numbers; server can retransmit missing ranges on request.
Protect tokens with HMAC‑signed timestamps; reject messages with timestamps outside an acceptable window to stop replay attacks.
Assign each event a monotonically increasing sequence number per client session.
Store the last processed sequence number on client and server; drop any event with a lower or equal number.
When a client reconnects, include its last processed sequence number so the server can resend missing events.
Use a short‑lived signed token (e.g., JWT) with a nonce and expiration; verify it on each WebSocket upgrade.
Bind the token to the client’s IP or TLS session (token binding) to prevent replay from other hosts.
Enable TLS (wss://) to protect confidentiality and integrity of the WebSocket frames.
Optionally add a per‑message HMAC using a shared secret to detect tampering if TLS termination is off‑loaded.
Implement a server‑side deduplication cache keyed by (client_id, sequence_id) for a limited window to handle duplicates.
Assign each message a monotonically increasing sequence number per client and drop any message with a lower number.
Include a server‑generated unique message ID and keep a short‑term cache to discard duplicates.
Use a signed token (e.g., JWT) with a short expiration and a per‑connection nonce to prevent replay.
Require TLS (wss://) for all WebSocket traffic and verify the Origin header to restrict hosts.
Validate the token on every new WebSocket upgrade and reject connections with missing or expired tokens.
Implement a sliding window of acceptable sequence numbers to tolerate out‑of‑order delivery while still detecting gaps.
Make message handling idempotent so that processing a duplicate does not cause side effects.
Rotate signing keys regularly and revoke compromised tokens via a revocation list checked on each message.
